mapping.a_element: path integral approach for calculating work distribution in a nonequilibrium steady state
mapping.b_element: Ruelle's principle (SRB distribution) for describing nonequilibrium stationary states in many-particle systems

mapping.a_element: dragged massive Brownian particle model (nonequilibrium system)
work distribution function in laboratory and comoving frames
mapping.b_element: statistical predictions for nonequilibrium stationary states (e.g., in sheared fluids)

mapping.a_element: asymptotic fluctuation theorem for work fluctuations
mapping.b_element: SRB distribution as a general principle for nonequilibrium steady states

mapping.a_element: time oscillatory behavior in work distribution for masses above a critical value
mapping.b_element: specific predictions derived from SRB without explicit construction (e.g., agreement with computer experiments)

brief_justification: While both papers address nonequilibrium steady states, Paper A uses a path integral approach to derive work fluctuations in a specific model (Brownian particle), focusing on inertial effects and fluctuation theorems. Paper B applies Ruelle's principle (SRB distribution) to many-particle systems, emphasizing statistical mechanics predictions without explicit dynamical equations. The underlying machinery differs: path integrals vs. ergodic theory, and the role of inertial effects in A has no direct counterpart in B.
